text: PREPARATION OF ENRICHING SALT $^{7}\mathrm{LiF}^{-233}\mathrm{UF}_{4}$ FOR REFUELING THE MOLTEN SALT REACTOR
6. $7_{\mathrm{LiF}} - 233_{\mathrm{UF}_4}$ Process Operations 42
6.10 Transfer of $^{7}\mathrm{LiF} - ^{233}\mathrm{UF}_4$ Salt Product to the MSRE 62
The Molten Salt Reactor has been refueled with an enriching salt concentrate, 7LiF-233UF4 (73-27 mole %).
Its preparation in a shielded cell was required because of the high 232U content (222 ppm) of the 233U.
The eutectic mixture, LiF- $\mathrm{UF_4}$ (73-27 mole %), was purified by treatment with hydrogen, which reduced the corrosion products to metal and subsequently allowed their removal by filtration.
In July 1966, an ad hoc committee was appointed to study the feasibility of substituting $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ for the $^{235}\mathrm{U}$ being used to fuel the Molten Salt Reactor.
It was the recommendation of this committee that a charge of eutectic salt containing $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ be prepared for enriching the MSRE prior to removal of the $^{235}\mathrm{U}$ from the system.

A single-step process, in which the fuel concentrate could be prepared directly from $^{233}\mathrm{UO}_3$ and LiF, in a single reaction vessel located in a hot cell, was considered to be the simplest and most economical approach.
Biological shielding would be required because of the high $^{232}\mathrm{U}$ content (222 ppm) of the $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ feed material.

This report summarizes the preliminary phases of the work - development, design, and construction - as well as the actual operation of the process used to prepare the $7_{\text{LiF}}.233_{\text{UF}_4}$ (73-27 mole %) eutectic salt.

The substitution of $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ for $^{235}\mathrm{U}$ in the MSRE had to be considered from three standpoints: (1) the nuclear characteristics of the $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ system, (2) the chemistry of the circulating fuel, and (3) the preparation and handling of a $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ salt containing $^{232}\mathrm{U}$ .

(1) Critical loadings would provide a check on the available nuclear data on $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ for predicting critical conditions in a reactor with a neutron energy spectrum similar to that of the proposed molten salt breeder reactor (MSBR).
(2) Measurement of $^{234}\mathrm{U}$ -to- $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ atom ratios over a period of substantial burnup might further evaluate available nuclear data and calculational methods.

(4) Knowledge of the fission product yields for $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ , as compared with those for $^{235}\mathrm{U}$ , would provide a means of more positively identifying the reactivity transients that follow changes in power levels.
The high $232_{\mathrm{U}}$ content of the $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ will make remote processing of a $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ fuel charge mandatory.

The preparation of the enriching salt, $^{7}$ LiF. $^{233}$ UF $_4$ (73-27 mole %), from $^{7}$ LiF and $^{233}$ UO $_3$ by a direct high-temperature, one-step process, was investigated in the laboratory and found to yield a satisfactory product.

However, during the cold run in Building 7930, the high temperatures necessary for this process were found to promote corrosion of the equipment; thus, an alternative method - a low-temperature, two-step process - was adopted.

Necessary modifications included: (1) the use of a vertically mounted, cylindrical reaction vessel rather than trays or fluidized-bed reactors, (2) the addition of LiF to the initial charge of material, and (3) the operation of the process at temperatures sufficient to maintain the LiF in its molten state.
Sintering: Helium atmosphere at $900^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ $\mathrm{UO}_{3} \rightarrow \mathrm{UO}_{2.6} + 0.2 \mathrm{O}_{2}$
Hydrogen at $900^{\circ}C$ $\mathrm{UO}_{2.6} + 0.6\mathrm{H}_{2}\rightarrow \mathrm{UO}_{2} + 0.6\mathrm{H}_{2}\mathrm{O}$
Hydrofluorination: $\mathrm{H}_{2}-\mathrm{HF}$ sparge at $900-550^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ $\mathrm{UO}_{2} + 4 \mathrm{HF} \rightarrow \mathrm{UF}_{4} + 2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$
Reduction of impurities: Hydrogen sparge at $700^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ $\mathrm{MF}_{2} + \mathrm{H}_{2} \rightarrow \mathrm{M}^{\circ} + 2\mathrm{HF}$
produced, the liquidus temperature of the fluoride components decreased from 845 to $490^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , the melting point of the eutectic mixture.
In the equipment used in the development runs, the liquidus temperature could be measured and the $\mathbf{U}\mathbf{F}_{4}$ concentration could be determined by comparison with the phase diagram.
A final treatment of the melt with hydrogen at $700^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ reduced the concentration of nickel and iron to acceptably low levels.

The facility is divided into four major areas: (1) an office area adjacent to, but isolated from, areas that contain radioactivity, (2) an operating area with a development laboratory, chemical makeup area, and equipment rooms for service equipment, (3) a maintenance operating area with service areas for receiving and storing spent fuels, and (4) a cell complex containing seven hot cells (six shielded and one unshielded).
The $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ fuel charge for the MSRE was prepared in cell G.
The interior of this cell is 20 ft wide, 16 ft long, and 30 ft high.

This decreased the effective height of the cell to 22 ft, which, in reality, was further reduced to 14 ft of effective headroom because of the in-cell electromechanical manipulator system space requirements at the top of the cell.
1) are built into the walls of the cell; four of these are equipped with viewing windows, and two have window forms that are filled with removable shielding.
There are more than 100 penetrations into the cell for process services - many more than are required for the salt preparation.
The cell ventilation system normally maintains a pressure of $-5$ in. $\mathsf{H}_2\mathsf{O}$ and a flow of 1033 cfm of air through the cell; the vessel off-gas system removes
25 cfm of air from the cell and process vessels and routes it to a system that is maintained at a pressure of -17 in. $\mathsf{H}_2\mathsf{O}$ .
The roof of the cell contains a hatch with a 10- by 6-ft opening that is sealed and shielded.
The hatch provides access to the cell with the 50-ton building crane.
A Program and Remote Systems Model 3000 electromechanical manipulator system is mounted on rails to give complete coverage of the cell.

section: 4.1.2 Radioactive Hot Drain - Hot Off-Gas System (RHD-HOG)
text: Liquid waste is separated from the off-gas and is collected in tank B-2-T in the below-grade and shielded waste tank pit.

section: 4.1.2 Radioactive Hot Drain - Hot Off-Gas System (RHD-HOG)
text: With the HOG system pressure controlled at -17 in. $\mathsf{H}_2\mathsf{O}$ , the flow of air from the cell was adjusted to 25 cfm by a manually operated valve.
Approximately 1033 cfm of air enters cell G from the cell G pump room via a series of filters, a fire damper, a back-flow preventer, and a cell pressure control valve.

The exhaust is routed to the filter pit, where it passes through two sets of filters in series; from there, it passes through a 30-in. duct to the centrifugal blowers and into the 250-ft HFIR stack for dispersion into the atmosphere.
The controls on the ventilation system maintain a pressure of -5 in. $\mathsf{H}_2\mathsf{O}$ in the cell under normal conditions.
Pressures as low as -20 in. $\mathsf{H}_2\mathsf{O}$ or as high as -1 in. $\mathsf{H}_2\mathsf{O}$ can be maintained in the cell under certain emergency conditions.
Only a failure of all emergency systems would allow the pressure in the cell to rise above -0.5 in. $\mathsf{H}_2\mathsf{O}$ .

The shielding of the TURF is designed in such a manner that, during operation with radioactive material having an intensity of $10^{5}$ r/hr, the penetrating dose rates in normally occupied areas are no greater than 0.25 mrem/hr, with small hot spots no greater than 2.5 mrem/hr.

To satisfy the allowable design radiation levels, the operating cells have $5 - 1 / 2$ ft-thick walls of normal concrete up to a height of 11 ft, $4 - 1 / 2$ ft of normal concrete for the remaining portions of the vertical walls, and 5-ft-thick concrete on the roof.
This amount of biological shielding was more than adequate to reduce exposure doses rates to $< 0.1\mathrm{mr / hr}$ .
Each window is a composite unit consisting of 7 in. of glass and 58 in. of zinc bromide solution; it is well sealed to minimize leakage of air around its periphery.
A Programmed and Remote Systems Model 3000 Manipulator System is installed on a set of rails in cell G.
The tube hoist on this manipulator has a vertical travel of $13 - 1 / 2$ ft and a lifting capacity of 1000 lb.
A grip force of 200 lb can be exerted with the fingers.
The Model A and the Model D manipulator have maximum lift capabilities of 25 lb and 100 lb, respectively.
Two processes were developed for the preparation of the fuel concentrate: (1) a high-temperature process, and (2) a low-temperature process.
(3) Conversion of the oxide to $\mathbf{U}\mathbf{F}_{4}$ by hydrofluorination and concurrent dissolution of the $\mathbf{U}\mathbf{F}_{4}$ in the molten LiF to form the eutectic mixture.
The $7_{\text{LiF}}$ was combined with the $^{233}\mathrm{UO}_3$ in the initial charge to the reaction vessel.
The reduction process was then conducted at temperatures in excess of $845^{\circ}\text{C}$ (LiF melting point) so that the oxide particles were suspended in the molten fluoride.
the furnace temperature controller was adjusted to approximately $50^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ above this value.
3) was used to produce the fuel concentrate.
It differs in only one major respect from that used in the production of the original $235\mathrm{U}$ -bearing enriching salt for the MSRE.
The oxide is digested at $550^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ and then reduced to $\mathrm{UO}_2$ by treatment with hydrogen at temperatures ranging from 400 to $550^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .
The oxide is then converted to $\mathrm{UF}_4$ by hydrofluorination at temperatures ranging from 400 to $630^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ and at HF (in hydrogen) concentrations varying from 5 to $40\%$ .

The scrubbers, namely, four 13-gal polyethylene bottles filled with $10\%$ KOH, were connected in series and fitted with the necessary fill-and-drain connections.
Oxide Can Lengthening Equipment. - The $^{233}\mathrm{UO}_3$ decanning station was designed by using can drawings and sample cans from the Savannah River Laboratory.
After the decanner was fabricated, it was discovered that the actual cans were 1-3/4 in. shorter than denoted on the drawings.

(The measurements of the actual cans had not been checked closely because of the high radiation levels of the oxide.) Since modification of the decanner was impractical, we decided to lengthen each can 1-1/2 in. by cementing an extension on it.

15, were required to accomplish this: (1) a machining fixture to bevel the end of the can, (2) a press to force the extension onto the can, and (3) a fixture to cure the epoxy resin used in the cemented joint.

In-Cell Titration Assembly. - The off-gas titration assembly consisted of a train of three 3-in.-diam by 6-in.-tall glass vessels, a wet test meter for measuring gas flows, and the necessary tubing and valves to permit reagents to be added and flushed from outside the cell.

A 24-kw, 12-in.-cavity furnace was used for the reaction vessel; a 7-1/2-kw, 4-7/8-in.-cavity unit was used for the transfer vessel; and a 10-kw, 4-7/8-in.-cavity unit was used for the shipping container assemblies.

Enrichment Capsule Drilling and Weighing Fixture. - Two holes were drilled in each of 45 enrichment capsules to permit the salt to flow out when the capsule is immersed in the salt in the reactor pump bowl.

16); here, a bottom outlet hole and a top vent hole were drilled in the capsule, the capsule was weighed, the lifting bail was tested for integrity, and the capsule was inserted into the shipping carrousel.
17) was installed to accommodate the product shipping container disassembly, the shipment preparation operations, and the capsule drilling and weighing fixture.
Operating procedures were developed for each of the 25 separate operations required for the fuel production.

Review and revision, on a day-to-day basis, continued throughout the $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ production runs; the minor equipment failures and malfunctions that occurred during these runs led to extensive procedural changes to ensure continued production of a high-quality fuel concentrate.

In this run, $14.6\mathrm{kg}$ of uranium oxide (11.6 kg of $^{238}\mathrm{U}$ ) plus $3.4\mathrm{kg}$ of LiF were charged to the system to produce 18.7 kg of $\mathrm{LiF} \cdot \mathrm{UF}_4$ (73-27 mole %) eutectic.[5]

The $\mathrm{UO}_3$ had been processed at the Y-12 Plant, and the empty inner and outer cans had been obtained from the Savannah River Laboratory.
Chemical operations began on January 24, 1968, with the sintering operation, which was conducted at $900^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ with a helium flow of 1 std liter/min through the bed.
A 2-liter/min flow of gas, consisting initially of 0.1 liter of hydrogen and 1.9 liters of helium per minute, was attained initially.
Then the flow rate was increased, in increments, to 10 liters/min, where it was maintained for 5 hr.
The conversion of the oxide to the fluoride began January 27, 1968.
Anhydrous hydrogen fluoride mixed with hydrogen (10 meq of HF per liter of hydrogen) was injected through a dip line to the bottom of the $\mathrm{UO}_2$ bed at the rate of 2.2 liters/min.
This reaction began at $900^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , and the temperature was periodically decreased to maintain the melt at about $50^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ above the liquidus temperature.
The data obtained with the new equipment showed that the utilization of HF was essentially zero (which indicated that the conversion of the oxide was complete) after 295 hr of hydrofluorination.
After 250 hr of additional HF treatment, the dip line became plugged at a location about $1-3/4$ in. from its open end.

The effluent gases from the reaction vessel were directed to a caustic scrubbing system, which consisted of polyethylene bottles filled with $20\%$ KOH solution and Raschig rings (to prevent collapse).

Frequent sparging to promote mixing, frequent recharging of the caustic solution, rinsing with hot water, and a decrease (from $20\%$ to $10\%$ ) in KOH concentration permitted use of the system for the remainder of the cold run.

The product was filtered into the intermediate storage vessel; then it was transferred to the capsules and shipping containers by subjecting the salt to a helium overpressure of approximately 12 to 15 psig and forcing it out of the vessel through a dip line.
(4) use of a high-temperature thread lubricant on all fittings,
(6) training operators in use of the manipulators on a simulated piping arrangement.

6. $7_{\mathrm{LiF}} - 233_{\mathrm{UF}_4}$ PROCESS OPERATIONS
Three production batches, using the low-temperature flowsheet, were required in order to prepare $63.4\mathrm{kg}$ of the fuel-enriching concentrate, ${}^{7}\mathrm{LiF}-{}^{233}\mathrm{UF}_{4}$ .
This concentrate contained $39\mathrm{kg}$ of uranium (35.6 kg of $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ ).
The first run began May 9, 1968, and the third run was completed July 30, 1968.

The 45 enrichment capsules were filled with a portion of the first-run product; the four $7\mathrm{-kg}$ shipping containers were filled with the remainder of the first-run product, all of the second-run product, and a portion of the third-run product; the miscellaneous shipping container assembly was filled with the remainder of the third-run product.
The ten salt shipping containers and the 45 enrichment capsules were delivered to the Molten Salt Reactor as required in the reactor enrichment schedule.

The LiF feed $(99.97\%)$ had previously been densified to 1.2 g/cm³ by hydrogen treatment at elevated temperatures.

section: 6.2 Radiation Levels of the Oxide Feed
text: The high radiation levels of the oxide feed material (see Table 4) result from the daughter activity of the $^{232}\mathrm{U}$ (222 ppm) in the $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ .

section: 6.2 Radiation Levels of the Oxide Feed
text: From the shielding standpoint, the 2.6-Mev gamma radiation from the $^{208}\mathrm{Tl}$ and the 2.2-Mev gamma radiation from the $^{212}\mathrm{Bi}$ are the most important.

The $^{233}\mathrm{UO}_3$ had been packaged in double-walled aluminum containers, containing approximately $450\mathrm{g}$ of uranium.
OD by 8 in. long, had been sealed on one end by welding, and on the other end by a magnetic forming technique.
They had been stored in Building 3019 at ORNL.
To prepare the $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ fuel concentrate, we removed 89 cans from the facility and transported them, in six loads, to the TURF in a shielded carrier.
Three separate operations were required to elongate the cans to the 9-1/2-in. length required for proper operation of the can opening box.

This cap was installed on the can in a pressing fixture, and then the can (with cap) was placed in a curing fixture, where five cans at a time were cured under longitudinal pressure for 1 hr at a temperature of 110 to $120^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .
(4) A vibrator shaft seal failed, leading to loss of oxide powder from the box.

The expanded bed of $^{233}\mathrm{UO}_3$ was heated for 2 hr at $550^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ in a helium atmosphere to remove, by pyrolysis, any traces of ammonium compounds or other volatiles remaining from the chemical processing.
The bed was then cooled to $400^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ before hydrogen treatment was started.
\mathrm {U O} _ {3} + \mathrm {H} _ {2} \rightarrow \mathrm {U O} _ {2} + \mathrm {H} _ {2} \mathrm {O} + 7 2, 0 0 0 \mathrm {c a l / g - m o l e}.
The concentration of hydrogen (in helium) was adjusted initially to 5 vol % and was gradually increased to 50 vol % during the first 4 hr of treatment.
The temperatures within the bed were monitored by an internal probe with 12 thermocouples that were placed at 2-in. intervals along the vertical axis of the bed.
The procedure of incrementally increasing the hydrogen concentration was repeated until the hydrogen:helium volume ratio was 1:l; the gaseous flow rate was 2 liters/min.
temperature of the lower zone during the first 8 hr of treatment is due to the exothermic reaction (the reaction vessel furnace was set to control at $400^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ ).

After the temperature excursion resulting from the initial increases in hydrogen flow had subsided (approximately 12 hr), the temperature of the furnace was increased, at the rate of $30^{\circ}\mathrm{C/hr}$ , until the bed temperature was $525 \pm 25^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .
The reduction operation was continued at this temperature, with 50 vol % hydrogen--50 vol % helium, until 50 to 100% excess over the stoichiometric amount of hydrogen had been passed through the bed.
Hydrogen utilization within the bed was $100\%$ until the reaction zone approached the top of the powder bed; then a slight decrease was observed.
Upon completion of the oxide reduction step, the bed was allowed to cool to $400^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .
The conversion of $\mathrm{UO}_2$ to $\mathrm{UF}_4$ by hydrofluorination, using HF gas diluted with hydrogen, began at $400^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ and was completed at $625^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .
The HF gas was supplied to the process by withdrawing it from the vapor space of a heated 100-lb HF cylinder.
The cylinder heater was thermostatically controlled to provide a vapor pressure to 12 to 14 psig.
The gas was passed through a maze of tightly packed nickel wire in a 2-in.-diam nickel tube that was maintained at $625^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ to remove sulfur from the stream.
At the beginning of the hydrofluorination step, the composition of the gas was 95 vol % hydrogen--5 vol % HF; the flow rate of the mixture was 2 std liters/min.
Over a period of 3 to 4 hr, the HF concentration
was incrementally increased to $40 \, \text{vol}\%$ as the exothermic reaction caused the bed temperature to rise from $400 \, \text{to} \, 450^{\circ}\text{C}$ .

After the HF concentration of the hydrofluorinating mixture had reached $40 \, \text{vol}\%$ the reaction zone traveled, in the form of a band, up through the bed (in a manner similar to that observed during the hydrogen reduction) as the $\mathrm{UO}_2$ was converted to $\mathrm{UF}_{14}$ .
\mathrm {U O} _ {2} + 4 \mathrm {H F} \rightarrow \mathrm {U F} _ {4} + 2 \mathrm {H} _ {2} \mathrm {O} + 1 4 4, 0 0 0 \mathrm {c a l / g - m o l e}

In runs 1 and 2, several furnace temperature adjustments were made during the first five days of treatment in an effort to increase the bed temperature to $475 - 500^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ (because the HF gas is less corrosive to nickel at the higher temperature).
In run 3, the furnace operated satisfactorily and did not require adjustment.
The HF utilization was essentially $100\%$ for the first five days and then decreased sharply as the reaction zone reached the top of the bed.
Then the temperature of the bed was increased to $625^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , where it was held for two days to ensure completeness of the reaction.
A total of $13.5\mathrm{kg}$ of uranium, as $\mathrm{UO}_2$ , was converted to $\mathrm{UF}_4$ in each of the three runs; only very minor differences in the runs were noted.
The eutectic mixture $\mathrm{UF}_{4} = \mathrm{LiF}$ (27-73 mole %) (Fig.
22) was formed by adding the stoicoimetric quantity of lithium fluoride powder to the uranium tetrafluoride powder and fusing the mixture.

Dry LiF powder was added to the reaction vessel by the following series of operations: (1) it was poured into a hopper located on top of the decanning station, (2) it was dumped from the hopper into the decanner box, and (3) it was transferred from the box to the vessel by vibration and brushing.

The temperature of the reaction vessel containing the stratified $\mathrm{UF}_4$ and LiF powders was then increased to $855^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ in order to melt the lithium fluoride (mp, $835^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ ).

The melt was digested at this temperature for 3 hr while it was sparged with hydrogen (at a flow rate of 0.2 liter/min) to reduce any extraneous compounds that might have been introduced during the LiF addition.
A rapid cooldown to $650^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ was observed in the lower region of the vessel where liquid existed.
At the same time, temperatures as high as $850^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ were measured in the upper regions of the vessel where fusing of the compounds had not yet taken place.
The temperature of the pool eventually reached $850^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , the set point of the furnace temperature controller.
In runs 1 and 3, the $\mathrm{UF}_{4}$ and LiF had to be
LiF-UF<sub>4</sub> Phase Diagram.
heated to about $850^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ before melting occurred.
In run 2, initial melting occurred at $650^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , nearly $200^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ below the melting point of the lithium fluoride.
The low-temperature initial melting must have resulted from the presence of a sizable heel of salt (mp, $490^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ ) that remained in the reaction vessel from run 1.
Probably, this heel acted as a "seed" to permit fusing of the $\mathbf{U}\mathbf{F}_{4}$ and LiF powders at the lower temperature.

The 9-in.-deep pool of eutectic salt (mp, $490^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ ) was next treated with 20 vol % HF--80 vol % hydrogen (flow rate, 2.4 liters/min) for 24 hr at a temperature of $700^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ to remove the last traces of oxide from the salt prior to the hydrogen purification procedure.

(A 1/2-in.-OD x 2-1/4-in.-long nickel cup was immersed in the salt to withdraw a 25-g sample.) After the sample had solidified, a 1-in.-long section was cut from the center and analyzed petrographically and chemically for the presence of $\mathrm{UO}_2$ .

In each of the three runs, the $\mathrm{UO}_2$ content was reported to be less than the lower limit of accuracy (200 ppm) for the petrographic appraisal; thus, subsequent HF treatment was unnecessary.
Chemical analyses of the same samples showed oxide contents (in the product salts) of 62, 34, and 32 ppm for runs 1, 2, and 3 respectively.
The eutectic salt was purified by bubbling pure hydrogen gas (3 to 10 ppm $\mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{O}$ ), at a flow rate of 2 std liters/min, through the 10-in.-deep melt.
is $700^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .
The end point of the purification was evident from the leveling off of the HF evolution rate at 0.025 meq per liter of hydrogen.

The actual concentrations of these impurities in the enriching concentrate, as delivered to the MSRE, should be somewhat lower than the values reported because an additional 24 hr of hydrogen treatment was provided in each run after the filtered samples were withdrawn for analysis.
Table 6 gives the chemical analyses of the fuel shipments to the MSRE; Table 7 gives the isotopic analysis of the uranium.
During each of the three runs, the reaction vessel was exposed for 20 days to $40 \, \text{vol}\%$ HF--60 vol % hydrogen at temperatures ranging from 400 to $850^{\circ}\text{C}$ .
Approximately $5\, \text{g}$ of nickel (from the nickel liner of the reaction vessel) was lost to each melt.
This corresponds to a uniform corrosion rate of slightly less than 0.001 in./year - a low rate for this type of process.

Eight transfer operations were necessary to convey the three $13.5\mathrm{-kg}$ batches of eutectic salt mixture from the reaction vessel to the intermediate transfer vessel and then to the various shipping container assemblies.
The transfers were made by applying helium gas pressure over a pool of molten salt having a temperature of $600^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .

The transfers ranged in size from the $13.4\mathrm{kg}$ of uranium (4.7 liters of salt) in the production batches to $4.3\mathrm{kg}$ of uranium (1.5 liters of salt) that was required to fill the 45 enrichment capsule array.
The sintered nickel filter was 3 in. in diameter, and the pore size was $20\mu$ .
Transfers through this line required an overpressure of 15 psig to overcome the static head (18 ft $\mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{O}$ ) and the restriction to flow of the filter.
Time required to complete the transfer varied from 78 to 140 min.
They were made via a 1/4-in.-OD nickel transfer line that connected the assembly to a dip line in the storage vessel.
Since filters were not involved in these transfers, the transfers were completed in approximately 10 min.
The transfer operations were conducted at a salt temperature of $600^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .

section: 6.9 Container Disassembly and Preparation for Shipment
text: The 45 enriching capsules were clipped and trimmed, were tested (i.e., their lifting balls were tested) with a 15-lb pull, were drilled for draining and venting, and were identified and weighed.

text: The 2-1/2-in.-diam product cans were easily removed from the filling arrays; however, removal of the top and bottom plugs from each can was quite difficult.

A total of $39.72\mathrm{kg}$ of uranium, in the form of uranium oxide powder, was transferred to cell G of the TURF.
Of this amount, $38.95\mathrm{kg}$ of uranium, as $\mathrm{LiF - UF_4}$ , was packaged as the finished product.
The can-opening operation accounted for a loss of $245\mathrm{g}$ of uranium as uranium oxide.
Most of this, $200\mathrm{g}$ , was lost through the empty can discharge chute because excessive powder had accumulated in the box.
A leaking seal on the vibratory screen drive shaft accounted for $45\mathrm{g}$ .
Remote modification to the decanning station and changes in operating procedures eliminated these sources of loss after the first batch (29 oxide cans) had been processed.
Sampling accounted for a loss of $100\mathrm{g}$ of uranium.
Two 15-g and two 5-g salt samples were required in each of three production runs.
The heel in the reaction vessel amounted to $27\mathrm{g}$ .
However, the storage vessel contained $346\mathrm{g}$ of uranium as heel as a result of a dip line replacement.
The dip line was installed improperly and, as a result, about $1/3$ in. of unrecoverable salt heel was left in the vessel.

The compression-type fittings used on the salt transfer lines and on the gas supply lines at the vessel nozzles were extremely difficult to make and break after they had been thermally cycled from room temperature to $600 - 700^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .
17) designed for the product container and capsule array disassembly operations served well in these functions and was also used extensively as a general-purpose work bench.
Altogether, more than 300 photographs were made.

Fifty-five detailed, step-by-step maintenance procedures were written by using the photographs and field sketches (more than 300) that had been used in the fabrication and installation of the equipment.
Reactor-grade eutectic salt concentrate, $^{7}$ LiF- $^{233}$ UF4 (73-27 mole %), can be prepared by remote means from LiF and $^{233}$ UO2 or $^{233}$ UF4 by the process described in this report.
Concentrations of the corrosion products nickel and iron can be reduced to satisfactorily low levels by hydrogen treatment of moltens salt at $550^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .

A binary mixture of $\mathrm{UF}_{4}$ -LiF having a eutectic composition can be fused at a temperature of $650^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ in the presence of a small amount of liquid eutectic heel remaining in a vessel.
In the absence of the liquid heel, it is necessary to heat the materials to the melting point of the LiF $(835^{\circ}\mathrm{C})$ to achieve liquefaction.

To obtain a product containing less than $40~\mathrm{ppm}$ of oxygen and to avoid plugging of dip lines by the formation of oxides within them, it was necessary to install a catalytic recombiner and a molecular-sieve drying column in the hydrogen supply system.
The use of Lindetype 414 l/16-in.-diam sieve material in a regenerative drying column reduced the moisture content of commercial-grade hydrogen to less than 3 ppm.
